note_html:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Gregory, has a non-healing foot ulcer on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, which is approximately 1x2 inches in size. It is red, draining, and has some edema around it. The patient reports that it throbs and is concerned about its healing. The patient has had the ulcer for about two months, with recent worsening of symptoms. The patient attributes the onset of the ulcer to walking barefoot on the beach at Dewey Beach. The patient's primary concern is the drainage and redness of the ulcer. The patient has not taken the antibiotics prescribed by their primary care physician (PCP) yet.

Past medical history: The patient has diabetes, with an estimated A1C of 10. The patient takes medication for diabetes but does not regularly check their blood sugar levels. The patient's PCP has not created a treatment plan for their diabetes.

Review of systems: The patient denies fever, but has some throbbing pain in the foot.

Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]

Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are normal, with no fever.

Physical exam: The patient has a 1x2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the fifth MTP joint. The wound is red, draining, and has some edema around it. There is fluid coming out of the wound, but no necrosis, odor, or bony exposure. The patient feels increased pressure when the wound is touched, but it is not distinct pain.

Test Results: An X-ray of the right foot shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with signs of infection, which is not healing due to poor blood supply.

Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; suspected infection; uncontrolled diabetes; [NOT MENTIONED]

Plan:
I ordered an ABI (ankle-brachial index) to determine the blood supply to the foot. I performed a debridement to remove dead tissue from the wound. I prescribed clindamycin 400mg twice daily for 7 days to treat the infection. I will provide a surgical shoe for the patient to wear to reduce pressure on the foot and promote healing. I will see the patient back in two weeks to assess the wound's progress and possibly perform another debridement.

Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me in two weeks to assess the wound's progress. The patient is encouraged to call the office if they experience any issues or have any concerns.
